{{distinguish|a new theory of sex}}
[[File:Antisociality and Prosociality.svg|thumb|Diagram of traits associated with Antisociality and Prosociality and the two categories]]
Antisociality is a proposed alternative to the [[dark triad]] developed by [[Immanuelle]]. The main elements of it that differentiate it are that antisociality attempts to operate under a much more biological lens instead of a psychological lens. Antisociality is seen as complimentary to prosociality which is essentially the same traits expressed in reverse. 

This covers individual evil, for collective evil see the article [[collective evil]] which is an analogous one

Antisociality has two elements to it inspired by the [[W:Psychopathy Checklist|Psychopathy Checklist]]

* Factor 1 antisociality: associated with temperament, possibly dopamine receptor polymorphisms<ref name=":3" />, and bedtime. Likely impairs [[desire synthesis]] resulting in a lack of or slower development of a complex moral worldview and a weakened [[Agent-Arena relationship]]. It is selected for merely on the basis of a fast life history strategy and is not a response to pathology. Spiritual interventions are likely a useful tool for this to help people develop a [[neurological relationship with God]]{{Original Research}}
* Factor 2 antisociality: associated with the [[W:Terrie Moffitt's developmental theory of crime|developmental theory of crime]], tissues are disordered resulting in a shorter lifespan, and the result is a forced shift to fast life history strategies. This is the result of poor maternal health and is more linked to violence than factor 1. This is more linked to schizotypy than psychopathy. Nanotech interventions to fix tissue disorder are likely the best way to address factor 2 antisociality. 



This page was originally a general psychological information page, and then turned into a page on this theory alongside trying to codify [[eusexuality]]. Antisociality/Prosociality are both strongly linked to sexuality, as they are essentially reproductive strategies, but eusexuality isn't exhausted by Antisociality-Prosociality and discussing it on this page makes the page too large in scope, as such work has been moved to the page [[a new theory of sex]].

 

It is based on a biological theory and the notion of two separate factors derived from the hare psychopathy scale

Factor 1 Antisociality: Temperamental in origin based on fast life history strategy. Genetic and not strongly associated with violence, associated with bedtimes.

Factor 2 Antisociality: Associated with Fluctuating Asymmettry, poor health, and a reaction of a fast life history strategy in response to this.

* i.e. this is a meta-reaction to an observed bodily form.
* It is not a normal evolved response. More like a panic reaction "oh shit I will not be able to take care of my child in my old age, I guess I must have a ton of babies instead to hope one survives"

== Is Prosociality the same thing as morality ==
It is not. One idea is that prosociality is the thing that makes you follow *an* ethical system but not necessarily a particular one or the right ethical system

=== Unethical prosocial people ===
People who are prosocial can be unethical by following the wrong ethical system

=== Ethical antisocial people ===
Antisocial people being ethical is a bit more controversial. It's a possibility though that they can effectively only act on their antisocial drives in ways that are ethically neutral or [[suberogatory]]. Casual sex, even in a manipulative manner is not necessarily ethically wrong for example.

== Misc notes ==
Kind and Wicked learning environments<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The Two Settings of Kind and Wicked Learning Environments|url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0963721415591878|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|date=2015-10|issn=0963-7214|pages=379–385|volume=24|issue=5|doi=10.1177/0963721415591878|language=en|first=Robin M.|last=Hogarth|first2=Tomás|last2=Lejarraga|first3=Emre|last3=Soyer}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=David Epstein on kind and wicked learning environments|url=https://www.driverlesscrocodile.com/books-and-recommendations/david-epstein-on-kind-and-wicked-learning-environments/|website=Driverless Crocodile|date=2020-01-18|access-date=2022-11-05|language=en-GB|first=Stuart|last=Patience}}</ref>

General notes<ref>{{Cite web |title=APA PsycNet |url=https://psycnet.apa.org/fulltext/2014-01529-012.html |access-date=2022-03-25 |website=psycnet.apa.org |language=en}}</ref>

Autism and sociosexuality article<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Del Giudice |first=Marco |last2=Klimczuk |first2=Amanda C. E. |last3=Traficonte |first3=Daniel M. |last4=Maestripieri |first4=Dario |date=2014-09-01 |title=Autistic-like and schizotypal traits in a life history perspective: diametrical associations with impulsivity, sensation seeking, and sociosexual behavior |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1090513814000580 |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |language=en |volume=35 |issue=5 |pages=415–424 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2014.05.007 |issn=1090-5138}}</ref>

Dopamine receptor polymorphism<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Eisenberg |first=Dan T.A. |last2=Campbell |first2=Benjamin |last3=MacKillop |first3=James |last4=Modi |first4=Meera |last5=Dang |first5=David |last6=Lum |first6=J. Koji |last7=Wilson |first7=David S. |date=2007-10-01 |title=Polymorphisms in the Dopamine D4 and D2 Receptor Genes and Reproductive and Sexual Behaviors |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/147470490700500402 |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |language=en |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=147470490700500 |doi=10.1177/147470490700500402 |issn=1474-7049}}</ref>
[[Category:Sexuality]]
[[sn:Antisociality and Prosociality]][[Category:The Pride]]
[[Category:Alienation]]

== Dark triad and Big 5 articles ==
<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-05-05 |title=The Dark Triad of personality and its positioning in the Big Five model and on the Interpersonal Circumplex |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220505184406/https://files.osf.io/v1/resources/snp26/providers/osfstorage/58dd2516594d9001f697527d?action=download&direct&version=1 |access-date=2022-05-05 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref>

All the Dark Triad and Some of the Big Five Traits are Visible in the Face<ref>[https://psyarxiv.com/c3ngz/download Alper, S., Bayrak, F., & Yilmaz, O. (2021). All the Dark Triad and some of the Big Five traits are visible in the face. Personality and Individual Differences, 168, 110350. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2020.110350] </ref> This suggests people can biologically recognize antisocialirty and avoid it, and likewise are drawn to prosociality for biological reasons.

<ref>https://www.researchgate.net/publication/353331911_Big_Five_and_Dark_Triad_personality_traits_as_predictors_of_multicultural_attitude_and_efficacy</ref>

== Fast life history strategy vs Criminal phenotype ==
Criminal phenotype is always fast life history, but fast life history can be peaceful. 

Having a sufficiently dysgenic phenotype with high fluctuating asymmetry and minor physical anomalies leads to the development of a particularly venomous fast strategy which is criminality. 

== Factor 1 (Sociosexuality) ==
Individuals who are sociosexually unrestricted tend to score higher on [[openness to experience]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lameiras Fernández |first1=M. |last2=Rodríguez Castro |first2=Y. |year=2003 |title=The Big Five and sexual attitudes in Spanish students |journal=Social Behavior and Personality |volume=31 |issue=4 |pages=357–362 |doi=10.2224/sbp.2003.31.4.357}}</ref> and be more [[Extraversion and introversion|extraverted]],<ref name="Wright">{{cite journal |last1=Wright |first1=T. M. |year=1999 |title=Female sexual behavior: Analysis of Big Five trait facets and domains in the prediction of sociosexuality |journal=Dissertation Abstracts International: Section B: The Sciences & Engineering |volume=59 |page=5611}}</ref> less [[Agreeableness|agreeable]],<ref name="Wright" /> lower on [[honesty-humility]],<ref>{{cite journal |last=Bourdage |first=J.S. |author2=Lee, K. |author3=Ashton, M.C. |author4=Perry, A. |year=2007 |title=Big Five and HEXACO model personality correlates of sexuality |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=43 |issue=6 |pages=1506–1516 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2007.04.008}}</ref> more [[Erotophilia|erotophilic]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schmitt |first1=D. P. |last2=Buss |first2=D. M. |year=2000 |title=Sexual dimensions of person description: Beyond or subsumed by the Big Five? |journal=Journal of Research in Personality |volume=34 |issue=2 |pages=141–177 |citeseerx=10.1.1.386.6601 |doi=10.1006/jrpe.1999.2267}}</ref> more [[Impulsivity|impulsive]],<ref name="Seal">{{cite journal |last1=Seal |first1=D. W. |last2=Agostinelli |first2=G. |year=1994 |title=Individual differences associated with high-risk sexual behaviour: Implications for intervention programmes |journal=AIDS Care |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=393–397 |doi=10.1080/09540129408258653 |pmid=7833356}}</ref> more likely to take risks,<ref name="Seal" /> more likely to have an avoidant [[Attachment theory|attachment style]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Brennan |first1=K. A. |last2=Shaver |first2=P. R. |year=1995 |title=Dimensions of adult attachment, affect regulation, and romantic relationship functioning |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=267–283 |doi=10.1177/0146167295213008}}</ref> less likely to have a secure [[Attachment theory|attachment style]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Simon |first1=E. P. |year=1997 |title=Adult attachment style and sociosexuality |journal=Dissertation Abstracts International: Section B: The Sciences & Engineering |volume=57 |page=5966}}</ref> and score higher on the [[Dark triad|Dark Triad]] traits (i.e. [[narcissism]], [[Machiavellianism (psychology)|Machiavellianism]], [[psychopathy]]).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Foster |first1=J. D. |last2=Shrira |first2=L. |last3=Campbell |first3=W. K. |year=2006 |title=Theoretical models of narcissism, sexuality, and relationship commitment |journal=Journal of Social and Personal Relationships |volume=23 |issue=3 |pages=367–386 |doi=10.1177/0265407506064204}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jonason |first1=P. K. |last2=Li |first2=N. P. |last3=Webster |first3=G. W. |last4=Schmitt |first4=D. P. |year=2009 |title=The Dark Triad: Facilitating short-term mating in men |journal=European Journal of Personality |volume=23 |pages=5–18 |citeseerx=10.1.1.650.5749 |doi=10.1002/per.698}}</ref> Higher masculinity<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Clark |first1=A. P. |year=2004 |title=Self-perceived attractiveness and masculinization predict women's sociosexuality |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=25 |issue=2 |pages=113–124 |doi=10.1016/s1090-5138(03)00085-0}}</ref> and [[Morningness-eveningness questionnaire|eveningness]]<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jankowski |first1=Konrad S |last2=Díaz-Morales |first2=Juan Francisco |last3=Vollmer |first3=Christian |last4=Randler |first4=Christoph |year=2014 |title=Morningness–eveningness and sociosexuality: Evening females are less restricted than morning ones |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=68 |pages=13–17 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2014.04.006}}</ref> in women is related to unrestricted sociosexuality. High [[self-monitoring]] is also associated with unrestricted sociosexuality, regardless of gender or sexual orientation.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sakaguchi |first1=K. |last2=Sakai |first2=Y. |last3=Ueda |first3=K. |last4=Hasegawa |first4=T. |year=2007 |title=Robust association between sociosexuality and self-monitoring in heterosexual and non-heterosexual Japanese |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=43 |issue=4 |pages=815–825 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2007.02.006}}</ref>

Individuals with an intrinsic religious orientation (i.e., religion as an end) tend to be sociosexually restricted, while those with an [[extrinsic religious orientation]] (i.e., religion as a means to achieve non-religious goals) tend to be unrestricted.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rowatt |first1=W.C. |last2=Schmitt |first2=D.P. |year=2003 |title=Associations between religious orientation and varieties of sexual experience |journal=Journal for the Scientific Study of Religion |volume=42 |issue=3 |pages=455–465 |citeseerx=10.1.1.550.8478 |doi=10.1111/1468-5906.00194}}</ref> Unrestricted women report engaging in more social interactions with men on a daily basis than restricted women. However, unrestricted individuals rate their interactions with their best friends (non-romantic) as lower in quality (i.e., as less pleasant and satisfying) than restricted individuals.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hebl |first1=M. R. |last2=Kashy |first2=D. A. |year=1995 |title=Sociosexuality and everyday social interaction |journal=Personal Relationships |volume=2 |issue=4 |pages=371–383 |doi=10.1111/j.1475-6811.1995.tb00099.x}}</ref>

=== Gender differences and sexual orientation ===
Men tend to have higher SOI scores and be more unrestricted than women across a variety of cultures.<ref name="FirstSchmitt">{{cite journal |last1=Schmitt |first1=D. P. |year=2005 |title=Sociosexuality from Argentina to Zimbabwe: A 48-nation study of sex, culture, and strategies of human mating |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |volume=28 |issue=2 |pages=247–311 |doi=10.1017/s0140525x05000051 |pmid=16201459}}</ref><ref name="Schmitt">{{cite journal |last1=Schmitt |first1=D. P. |year=2007 |title=Sexual strategies across sexual orientations: How personality traits and culture relate to sociosexuality among gays, lesbians, bisexuals, and heterosexuals |journal=Journal of Psychology and Human Sexuality |volume=18 |issue=2–3 |pages=183–214 |doi=10.1300/j056v18n02_06}}</ref> However, there is more variability in scores within each gender than between men and women, indicating that although the average man is less restricted than the average woman, individuals may vary in sociosexuality regardless of gender.<ref name="Gangestad">{{cite journal |last1=Gangestad |first1=S. W. |last2=Simpson |first2=J. A. |year=2000 |title=The evolution of human mating: Trade-offs and strategic pluralism |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=573–587 |doi=10.1017/s0140525x0000337x |pmid=11301543}}</ref>

[[Bisexuality|Bisexual]] women are significantly less restricted in their sociosexual attitudes than both [[lesbian]] and [[Heterosexuality|heterosexual]] women. Bisexual women are also the most unrestricted in sociosexual behavior, followed by lesbians and then, heterosexual women.<ref name="Schmitt" /> [[Homosexuality|Gay]] and bisexual men are similar to heterosexual men in sociosexual attitudes, in that they express relatively unrestricted attitudes relative to women. However, gay men are the most unrestricted in sociosexual behavior, followed by bisexual men and then, heterosexual men. This may be because gay men have more potential partners who prefer short-term, casual sexual encounters.<ref name="Schmitt" />

Unrestricted sociosexuality is associated with early life experiences with sex, more frequent [[Human sexual activity|sexual activity]] and a greater number of lifetime sex partners.  Unrestricted men tend to have greater [[rape myth]] acceptance, past sexual aggression and more conservative attitudes about women than restricted men. Unrestricted women tend to have more sexual fantasies involving having power or control over another person and lower levels of sexual conservatism than restricted women.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Yost |first1=M. R. |last2=Zurbriggen |first2=E. L. |year=2006 |title=Gender differences in the enactment of sociosexuality: An examination of implicit social motives, sexual fantasies, coercive sexual attitudes, and aggressive sexual behavior |journal=Journal of Sex Research |volume=43 |issue=2 |pages=163–173 |doi=10.1080/00224490609552311 |pmid=16817063}}</ref>

=== Hormones ===
Individuals who are partnered typically have lower [[testosterone]] levels than individuals who are single. However, this was found to apply solely to individuals who have a restricted sociosexuality. Partnered, unrestricted men and women's testosterone levels are more similar to the levels of single men and women.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Edelstein |first1=R.S. |last2=Chopik |first2=W.J. |last3=Kean |first3=E.L. |year=2011 |title=Sociosexuality moderates the association between testosterone and relationship status in men and women |url=http://edelsteinlab.psych.lsa.umich.edu/pubs/Edelstein%20et%20al%202011%20HB.pdf |journal=Hormones and Behavior |volume=60 |issue=3 |pages=248–255 |doi=10.1016/j.yhbeh.2011.05.007}}</ref>

=== Implications ===
Possessing an unrestricted sociosexuality seems to increase the likelihood of having a son by 12-19% in American samples.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kanazawa |first1=S. |last2=Apari |first2=P. |year=2009 |title=Sociosexually unrestricted parents have more sons: A further application of the generalized Trivers-Willard hypothesis (gTWH) |journal=Annals of Human Biology |volume=36 |issue=3 |pages=320–330 |doi=10.1080/03014460902766918 |pmid=19306221}}</ref> This may be explained by the generalized [[Trivers–Willard hypothesis|Trivers-Willard hypothesis]], which states that parents who possess any heritable trait that increases males' reproductive success above females'  will have more sons, and will have more daughters if they possess traits that increase females' reproductive success above males'.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kanazawa |first1=S |year=2005 |title=Big and tall parents have more sons: Further generalizations of the Trivers-Willard hypothesis |journal=Journal of Theoretical Biology |volume=235 |issue=4 |pages=583–590 |doi=10.1016/j.jtbi.2005.02.010 |pmid=15935175}}</ref> Since unrestricted sociosexuality increases the reproductive fitness of sons more than daughters (as males have the potential to have more offspring through casual sex), unrestricted parents have a higher-than-expected offspring sex ratio (more sons).

== Factor 2 (Fluctuating Asymmettry) ==

=== Developmental stability ===
Developmental stability is achieved when an organism is able to withstand genetic and environmental stress, to display the bilaterally symmetrical traits determined by its developmentally programmed phenotype.<ref name="Valen1962">{{cite journal |last=Valen |first=Leigh Van |date=June 1962 |title=A Study of Fluctuating Asymmetry |journal=Evolution |volume=16 |issue=2 |pages=125–142 |doi=10.2307/2406192 |jstor=2406192}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Markow |first=Therese Ann |date=1995-01-01 |title=Evolutionary Ecology and Developmental Instability |journal=Annual Review of Entomology |volume=40 |issue=1 |pages=105–120 |doi=10.1146/annurev.en.40.010195.000541 |pmid=<!--none-->}}</ref> To measure an individual's developmental stability, the FA measurements of 10 traits are added together, including ear width, elbows, ankles, wrists, feet, length of ears and fingers. This is achieved by: (L - R)trait 1 + (L - R)trait 2 + ......(L - R)trait 10. This provides a good overall measure of body FA, as every individual has some features that are not perfectly symmetrical.

Common environmental pressures leading to lower developmental stability include exposure to toxins, poison and infectious diseases, low food quality and malnutrition. Genetic pressures include spontaneous new mutations, and "bad genes" (genes that once had adaptive functions, but are being removed through evolutionary selection). A large fluctuating asymmetry (FA) and a low developmental stability suggests that an organism is unable to develop according to the ideal state of bilateral symmetry. The energy required for bilateral symmetry development is extremely high, making fully perfect bilateral symmetry functionally nonexistent in natural organic creatures. Energy is invested into survival in spite of the genetic and environmental pressures, before making bilaterally symmetrical traits. Research has also revealed links between FA and depression, genetic or environmental stress and measures of mate quality for sexual selection.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Graham |first1=J. H. |year=1992 |title=Genomic coadaption and development stability in hybrid zones |journal=Acta Zool. Fenn. |volume=191 |pages=121–132}}</ref><ref>Graham, J. H., D. C. Freeman and J. M. Emlen. 1993. Developmental stability: A sensitive indicator of populations under stress, pp. 1366158. /)I W. G. Landis, J. S. Hughes and M. A. Lewis (Eds.), Environmental Toxicology and Risk Assessment. American Society for Testing and Materials, Philadelphia, PA.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tomkins |first1=J. L. |last2=Simmons |first2=L. W. |year=1995 |title=Patterns of fluctuating asymmetry in earwig forceps: No evidence for reliable signalling |journal=Proc. Roy. Soc. Lond. B |volume=259 |issue=1354 |pages=89–96 |bibcode=1995RSPSB.259...89T |doi=10.1098/rspb.1995.0014 |s2cid=85238415}}</ref><ref>Moller, A. P. 1994. Sexual Selection and the Barn Swallow. Oxford University Press. Oxford.</ref>




One of the most consistent findings reported is tha'''t low FA is positively associated with measures of extraversion,''' suggesting that more symmetrical people tend to be more extraverted than less symmetrical individuals, particularly when specifying to symmetry within the face.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Fink |first1=Bernhard |last2=Neave |first2=Nick |last3=Manning |first3=John T. |last4=Grammer |first4=Karl |date=2005-08-01 |title=Facial symmetry and the 'big-five' personality factors |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=39 |issue=3 |pages=523–529 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2005.02.002}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pound |first1=Nicholas |last2=Penton-Voak |first2=Ian S. |last3=Brown |first3=William M. |date=2007-10-01 |title=Facial symmetry is positively associated with self-reported extraversion |url=http://bura.brunel.ac.uk/handle/2438/1786 |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=43 |issue=6 |pages=1572–1582 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2007.04.014}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Fink |first1=Bernhard |last2=Neave |first2=Nick |last3=Manning |first3=John T. |last4=Grammer |first4=Karl |date=2006-08-01 |title=Facial symmetry and judgements of attractiveness, health and personality |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=41 |issue=3 |pages=491–499 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2006.01.017}}</ref>




A correlation has also been reported between FA and human [[Dominance hierarchy|social dominance]].<ref>{{cite journal |last=Furlow |first=B |author2=Gangestad, SW |author3=Armijo-Prewitt, T |date=1998-01-07 |title=Developmental stability and human violence. |journal=[[Proceedings of the Royal Society B]] |volume=265 |issue=1390 |pages=1–6 |doi=10.1098/rspb.1998.0255 |pmc=1688754 |pmid=9470212}}</ref> However, '''research is proving less consistent with other personality factors''', with '''some finding some weak correlations between low FA and conscientiousness and openness to experience''', and '''others finding no significant differences between those with high or low FA'''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hope |first1=David |last2=Bates |first2=Timothy |last3=Penke |first3=Lars |last4=Gow |first4=Alan J. |last5=Starr |first5=John M. |last6=Deary |first6=Ian J. |date=2011-01-01 |title=Fluctuating Asymmetry and personality |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=50 |issue=1 |pages=49–52 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2010.08.020}}</ref>

=== Health-risk behaviours ===
It has been suggested that individuals with lower levels of FA may engage in more biologically costly behaviours such as recreational drug use<ref name="FA and recreational drug use">{{cite journal |last1=Borkowska |first1=Barbara |last2=Powlowski |first2=Boguslaw |date=2014 |title=Recreational drug use and fluctuating asymmetry: Testing the handicap principle |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=769–782 |doi=10.1177/147470491401200407 |pmid=25300053 |doi-access=free}}</ref> and risky body modifications such as piercings and tattoos.<ref name="FA and body modifications">{{cite journal |last1=Koziel |first1=Slawomir |last2=Kretschmer |first2=Weronika |last3=Pawlowski |first3=Boguslaw |date=May 2010 |title=Tattoos and piercings as signals of biological quality |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=31 |issue=3 |pages=187–192 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2009.09.009}}</ref> These ideas have been proposed in the context of Zahavi's handicap principle, which argues that highly costly behaviours or traits serve as signals of an organism's genetic quality.<ref name="Handicap principle">{{cite book |last1=Zahavi |first1=Amotz |url=https://archive.org/details/handicapprincipl0000zeha |title=The handicap principle: a missing piece of Darwin's puzzle |date=1997 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-510035-8 |location=Oxford |url-access=registration}}</ref> The relationship between FA and behaviours with high health risks has received mixed support. Individuals with body piercings and tattoos (which increase risk of blood-borne infections) have been shown to have lower levels of FA,<ref name="FA and body modifications" /> but individuals with lower FA do not engage in any more recreational drug use than those with higher FA levels.<ref name="FA and recreational drug use" />

=== Mental health in humans ===
Higher levels of FA have been linked to higher levels of some mental health difficulties. For instance, it has been shown that, among university students, higher FA is associated with higher levels of [[schizotypy]].<ref name="FA and schizotypy">{{cite journal |last1=Thoma |first1=Robert J |last2=Gangestad |first2=Steven W |last3=Euler |first3=Matthew J |last4=Lysne |first4=Per A |last5=Monnig |first5=Mollie |last6=Yeo |first6=Ronald A |date=2008 |title=Developmental instability and markers of schizotypy in university students |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=586–594 |doi=10.1177/147470490800600405 |doi-access=free}}</ref> [[Depression (mood)|Depression]] scores have been found to be higher in men, but not women, with higher levels of FA.<ref name="FA and depression study">{{cite journal |last1=Martin |first1=S. M. |last2=Manning |first2=J. T. |last3=Dowrick |first3=C. F. |date=May 1999 |title=Fluctuating asymmetry, relative digit length, and depression in men |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=203–214 |doi=10.1016/S1090-5138(99)00006-9}}</ref> One study by Shackelford and Larsen found that men and women with higher facial asymmetry reported more physiological complaints than those with lower facial asymmetry, and that both men and women with higher asymmetry experienced higher levels of psychological distress overall. For example, men with higher facial asymmetry experienced higher levels of depression compared to men with lower facial asymmetry.<ref name="FA and psychological, emotional and physiological distress">{{cite journal |last1=Shackelford |first1=Todd K |last2=Larsen |first2=Randy J |date=February 1997 |title=Facial asymmetry as an indicator of psychological, emotional and physiological distress |url=http://media.proquest.com/media/csa/psycarticles-set-c/PSP/72/psp_72_2_456.pdf.pdf?hl=&cit%3Aauth=Shackelford%2C+Todd+K.%3BLarsen%2C+Randy+J.&cit%3Atitle=Facial+asymmetry+as+an+indicator+of+psychological%2C+emotional%2C+and+physiological+distress.&cit%3Apub=Journal+of+Personality+and+Social+Psychology&cit%3Avol=72&cit%3Aiss=2&cit%3Apg=456&cit%3Adate=Feb+1997&ic=true&cit%3Aprod=PsycINFO&_%3D%3D&_s=AN3NIMckqXWhnjrcLomnr%2FMiehE%3D |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=72 |issue=2 |pages=456–466 |citeseerx=10.1.1.320.5882 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.72.2.456 |pmid=9107011 |access-date=19 February 2016}}</ref> Fluctuating asymmetry has also been studied in relation to [[psychopathy]]. One study looking at offenders and non-offenders found that, although offenders had higher levels of FA overall, psychopathic offenders had lower levels of FA compared to offenders who did not meet the criteria for psychopathy. Additionally, offenders with the highest levels of psychopathy were found to have similar levels of FA to non-offenders.<ref name="FA and psychopathy2">{{cite journal |last1=Lalumière |first1=Martin L. |last2=Harris |first2=Grant T. |last3=Rice |first3=Marnie E. |date=March 2001 |title=Psychopathy and developmental instability |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=75–92 |doi=10.1016/S1090-5138(00)00064-7 |pmid=11282307}}</ref>

=== Antisocial behaviours ===
Some studies suggest a link between FA and [[aggression]], but the evidence is mixed. In humans, criminal offenders show greater FA than nonoffenders.<ref name="FA and psychopathy">{{cite journal |last1=Lalumière |first1=Martin L. |last2=Harris |first2=Grant T. |last3=Rice |first3=Marnie E. |date=March 2001 |title=Psychopathy and developmental instability |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=75–92 |doi=10.1016/S1090-5138(00)00064-7 |pmid=11282307}}</ref> However, other studies report that human males with higher FA show less physical aggression<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Manning |first1=J. T. |last2=Wood |first2=D. |date=1998-03-01 |title=Fluctuating asymmetry and aggression in boys |journal=Human Nature |language=en |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=53–65 |doi=10.1007/s12110-998-1011-4 |issn=1045-6767 |pmid=26197357 |s2cid=20999908}}</ref><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last1=Furlow |first1=Bryant |last2=Gangestad |first2=Steven W. |last3=Armijo-Prewitt |first3=Tara |date=1998-01-07 |title=Developmental stability and human violence |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences |language=en |volume=265 |issue=1390 |pages=1–6 |doi=10.1098/rspb.1998.0255 |issn=0962-8452 |pmc=1688754 |pmid=9470212}}</ref> and less anger.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Muñoz-Reyes |first1=José Antonio |last2=Gil-Burmann |first2=Carlos |last3=Fink |first3=Bernhard |last4=Turiegano |first4=Enrique |date=2012-11-01 |title=Facial asymmetry and aggression in Spanish adolescents |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |volume=53 |issue=7 |pages=857–861 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2012.06.012}}</ref> Females show no association between FA and physical aggression,<ref name=":5" /> but some research has suggested that older female adolescents with higher facial FA are less hostile.<ref name=":6" /> The type of aggression being studied may account for the mixed evidence that is seen here. For example, one study found that females with higher FA demonstrated higher levels of reactive aggression in response to high levels of provocation, whereas high FA males showed more reactive aggression under low levels of provocation.<ref name="FA and reactive aggression">{{cite journal |last1=Benderlioglu |first1=Zeynep |last2=Sciulli |first2=Paul W. |last3=Nelson |first3=Randy J. |date=2004 |title=Fluctuating asymmetry predicts human reactive aggression |journal=American Journal of Human Biology |volume=16 |issue=4 |pages=458–469 |doi=10.1002/ajhb.20047 |pmid=15214064 |s2cid=17249167}}<!--|accessdate=28 March 2016--></ref>

Research is also mixed in other animals. In Japanese [[Panorpa|scorpionflies]] (''Panorpa nipponensis'' and ''Panorpa ochraceopennis''), FA differences between members of the same sex competing for food determines the outcome of [[Interspecific competition|interspecific contests]] and aggression better than body size or ownership of food.<ref name=":0">{{cite journal |last1=Thornhill |first1=R. |date=1992 |title=Fluctuating asymmetry and the mating system of the japanese scorpionfly, panorpa japonica. |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=44 |issue=5 |pages=867–879 |doi=10.1016/s0003-3472(05)80583-4 |s2cid=53176447}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Thornhill |first=Randy |date=1992-05-01 |title=Fluctuating asymmetry, interspecific aggression and male mating tactics in two species of Japanese scorpionflies |journal=Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology |language=en |volume=30 |issue=5 |pages=357–363 |doi=10.1007/BF00170603 |issn=0340-5443 |s2cid=20033857}}</ref> Furthermore, cannibalistic laying [[Chicken|hens]] (''Gallus gallus domesticus'') demonstrate more asymmetry than normal hens.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Yngvesson |first1=Jenny |last2=Keeling |first2=Linda J. |date=2001-03-01 |title=Body size and fluctuating asymmetry in relation to cannibalistic behaviour in laying hens |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=61 |issue=3 |pages=609–615 |doi=10.1006/anbe.2000.1616 |s2cid=53157959}}</ref> However, this link between FA and aggression in hens is questionable, as victimised hens also showed greater asymmetry. Furthermore, when [[Prenatal development|prenatally]] injecting hen eggs with excess [[serotonin]] (5-HT), the hens later exhibited more FA at 18 weeks of age, but displayed less aggressive behaviours. It is suggested that the stress introduced during early embryonic stages via certain factors (such as excess serotonin) may create developmental instability, causing phenotypic and behavioural variations (such as increased or decreased aggression).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dennis |first1=Rachel L. |last2=Fahey |first2=Alan G. |last3=Cheng |first3=Heng W. |date=2013-03-01 |title=Alterations to Embryonic Serotonin Change Aggression and Fearfulness |journal=Aggressive Behavior |language=en |volume=39 |issue=2 |pages=91–98 |doi=10.1002/ab.21459 |issn=1098-2337 |pmid=23386480}}</ref>

=== Factor 2.1 (Minor Physical Anomalies) ===
Moffitt writes, "[[Minor physical anomalies]], which are thought to be observable markers for hidden anomalies in neural development, have been found at elevated rates among violent offenders and subjects with antisocial [[personality traits]]."  [[Neural development]] in the [[fetus]] may also be affected by [[maternal]] [[drug abuse]], poor [[prenatal nutrition]], or [[prenatal]]/[[postnatal]] exposure to [[toxic]] agents.<ref name="developmental">Moffitt, Terrie E., 1993, Adolescence-Limited and Life-Course Persistent Antisocial Behavior: A Developmental Taxonomy. Psychological Review 100:674-701.</ref> Minor physical anomalies (MPAs) are features such as low-seated ears, furrowed tongue, and adherent ear lobes.  Evidence supporting the link between minor physical anomalies and antisocial behavior shows that the link only exists when adverse [[Social environment|environmental]] factors are present.<ref name="raine2">Raine, A. (2002). Biosocial studies of antisocial and violent behavior in children and adults: A review. Journal of abnormal child psychology, 30(4), 311-326.</ref>

== Autism (Factor 1 prosociality?) ==
Autism is likely linked to factor 1 prosociality in contrast with cluster B personality disorders, and may be a product of a slow life history strategy<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|title=Loneliness, social relationships, and a broader autism phenotype in college students|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886906004296|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|date=2007-06-01|issn=0191-8869|pages=1479–1489|volume=42|issue=8|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2006.10.021|language=en|first=Lisa E.|last=Jobe|first2=Susan|last2=Williams White}}</ref>. There is likely also a contrast with [[schizotypy]]<ref name=":1" /> which may indicate schizotypy is somewhat associated with factor 1 antisociality too.

High functioning autism is likely associated more with low on both antisocialities while low functioning autism is likely a product of high factor 2 low factor 1.

Autism is not a disability, it is a slower more rigorous development of social skills. Likely has no long term impacts at 30+ that aren't things like CPTSD or similar

Autism and sociosexuality article<ref name=":1" />

Misc notes<ref>https://www.researchgate.net/publication/327555165_Exploratory_Analysis_of_the_Links_among_Life_History_Reproductive_Strategy_Autism-Spectrum_Quotient_and_Quality_of_Life</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Del Giudice |first=Marco |date=2014-10-02 |title=An Evolutionary Life History Framework for Psychopathology |url=https://doi.org/10.1080/1047840X.2014.884918 |journal=Psychological Inquiry |volume=25 |issue=3-4 |pages=261–300 |doi=10.1080/1047840X.2014.884918 |issn=1047-840X}}</ref>

Autism leads to longer romantic relationships<ref name=":4" />. Paper says shorter friendships, but looking at the paper friendships were judged by criteria that looked more like it was judging when they made their first friends, so my interpretation is autistic people are better at maintaining friendships but worse at starting them especially when young

=== Autism and gender identity ===
Autism makes people more likely to be trans, idk if this is relevant to the topic though<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-09-14 |title=Largest study to date confirms overlap between autism and gender diversity |url=https://www.spectrumnews.org/news/largest-study-to-date-confirms-overlap-between-autism-and-gender-diversity/ |access-date=2022-04-01 |website=Spectrum {{!}} Autism Research News |language=en-US}}</ref>
==See Also ==

*[[A new theory of sex]]
==Related Wikipedia articles==
*[[w:Fluctuating asymmetry]]
*[[w:Minor physical anomalies]]
*[[w:Terrie Moffitt's developmental theory of crime]]
* [[w:Sociosexuality]]


[[Category:Inactive Essays]]
[[sn:Antisociality and Prosociality]][[Category:Psychological theories]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
[[sn:Antisociality and Prosociality]][[Category:Antisociality and Prosociality]]




[[Category:Theories]]
[[Category:Immanuelle]]

== References ==[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Pages]]
